outcomes.title: Integration Review
outcomes.description: Find the integral of a function using substitution and/or algebra.
outcomes.template:
Solve the following integrals. Make a note to yourself whether or not, we use substitution, substitution with some algebra replacement, or if substitution is not necessary.

\renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \int {{f0}}dx.

\renewcommand{\log}{\ln} {{v0}} {{u0}}. Then \renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \int {{f0}}dx = {{F0}}+C.

\renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \int {{f1}}dx.

\renewcommand{\log}{\ln} {{v1}} {{u1}}. Then \renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \int {{f1}}dx = {{F1}}+C.

\renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \int {{f2}}dx.

\renewcommand{\log}{\ln} {{v2}} {{u2}}. Then \renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \int {{f2}}dx = {{F2}}+C.

outcomes.title: Integration by Parts
outcomes.description: Find the integral of a function using integration by parts (and substitution when appropriate).
outcomes.template:
Solve the following integrals.

\renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \int_{ {{a1}} }^{ {{b1}} } {{fx}}dx.

\renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \int_{ {{a1}} }^{ {{b1}} } {{fx}}dx = {{Int1}}.

\renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \int {{gx}}dx.

By letting \renewcommand{\log}{\ln} u={{U}}, we have that \renewcommand{\log}{\ln} dx = {{dx}}du. Then by applying parts to the u integral we obtain: \renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \int {{gx}}dx = {{Int2}}+C.

outcomes.title: Integral of Trigonometric Functions
outcomes.description: Find the integral of trigonometric functions using identities, parts and/or substitution.
outcomes.template:
Solve the following integrals. Note that there are many ways to write the same trig expression via identities. Recall that \displaystyle \sin^2(\theta) = \frac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}, \cos^2(\theta) = \frac{1 + \cos(2\theta)}{2}.

\renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \int_{ {{a}} }^{ {{b}} } {{fx}}dx.

\renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \int {{fx}}dx.

\renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \int_{ {{a}} }^{ {{b}} } {{fx}}dx = \left({{Fx}}\right)\Big|_{ {{a}} }^{ {{b}} } ={{Int}}.

\renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \int {{fx}}dx = {{Fx}} +C.

outcomes.title: Trigonometric Substitution
outcomes.description: Find the integral of algebraic functions via trigonometric substitution.
outcomes.template:
Solve the following integrals. Note that there are many ways to write the same trig expression via identities.

\renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \int_{ {{lowx}} }^{ {{highx}} } {{fx}}dx.

\renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \int {{fx}}dx.

By letting x = {{sub}}, we obtain: \renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \int_{ {{lowx}} }^{ {{highx}} } {{fx}}dx = \int_{ {{lowt}} }^{ {{hight}} } {{ft}}d\theta =\left({{Ft}}\right)\Big|_{ {{lowt}} }^{ {{hight}} } ={{Int}}.

By letting x = {{sub}}, we obtain: \renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \int {{fx}}dx = \int {{ft}}d\theta = {{Ft}} +C ={{Fx}} +C.

outcomes.title: Partial Fractions
outcomes.description: Find the integral of rational functions via partial fraction decomposition.
outcomes.template:
Solve the following integrals using partial fraction decomposition and substitution when appropriate.

\renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \int \frac{ {{num}} }{ {{denom}} } dx.

Since \renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \frac{ {{num}} }{ {{denom}} } = {{PF}} We have that \renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \int \frac{ {{num}} }{ {{denom}} } dx = \int \left( {{PF}} \right)dx = {{Fx}} +C .

\renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \int {{f2x}}dx.

By letting \renewcommand{\log}{\ln} u={{U}}, we have that \renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \int {{f2x}}dx = {{F2x}}+C.

outcomes.title: Select a Strategy
outcomes.description: Identify the appropriate integration technique(s) and apply them to integrate a function.
outcomes.template:
Solve the following integrals. Note that there are many ways to write the same trig expression via identities.

\renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \int_{ {{lowx}} }^{ {{highx}} } {{fx}}dx.

\renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \int {{fx}}dx ={{Fx}} +C.
